title: Landscape Lighting Design & Installation
verdict: DIY Conditional. Low startup cost ($5-15K), strong margins (50-60% on installation), and growing demand from outdoor living trends. But seasonal demand (spring/summer), project-based revenue (no recurring unless maintenance add-on), and competition from general landscapers limit the upside.
verdict_detail: Landscape lighting sits at the intersection of electrical work and landscape design. Installers design and install outdoor lighting systems for residential and commercial properties. Revenue per project ranges from $2,500-15,000 for residential and $10,000-100,000+ for commercial. Material costs are 25-35% of project value, labor 20-30%, leaving 40-55% gross margins. The DIY path works because startup costs are low (tools, a truck, and inventory) and the work does not require an electrical license in most states for low-voltage systems (under 30 volts). The challenge is demand seasonality in cold-weather markets and the project-based revenue model with no natural recurring component unless you add maintenance contracts.
action: Start with a $5K pilot: install 5 residential systems in target market using low-voltage LED products. Test pricing, demand, and operational rhythm before scaling.
why_works: Low startup cost with no electrical license required for low-voltage. High project margins (40-55%). Growing demand from outdoor living and smart home trends. Visual, referral-friendly work (neighbors see the results). Maintenance contracts create recurring revenue potential.
why_fails: Seasonal demand in cold-weather markets (Nov-Mar dead). Project-based revenue is lumpy and unpredictable. General landscapers and electricians can offer lighting as an add-on, competing on price. Low barrier to entry means lots of competition. Design skill matters and is hard to learn quickly.
validate_first: Knock on 50 doors in an affluent neighborhood with existing landscape investments. Offer a free dusk demonstration (set up 3-4 temporary lights). Track conversion rate. If above 20%, demand is validated.
category_def: Landscape lighting design and installation provides outdoor illumination systems for residential and commercial properties. This includes pathway lighting, uplighting for trees and architecture, deck and patio lighting, security lighting, and smart-controlled systems. The work involves low-voltage (12V) systems that do not require an electrical license in most states, using LED fixtures, transformers, and burial-rated wire. Revenue comes from installation projects ($2,500-15,000 residential, $10K-100K+ commercial) with optional annual maintenance contracts ($300-800/year per property).
demand_drivers: Outdoor living trend: homeowners investing in outdoor spaces at record levels. Smart home integration: app-controlled lighting driving upgrades. LED technology: longer life, lower energy costs, and declining fixture prices make systems more affordable. Real estate value: well-designed landscape lighting increases curb appeal and property value. Security: outdoor lighting reduces liability and insurance costs.
geo_fit: Best in warm-climate markets where outdoor spaces are used year-round (Florida, Texas, Southwest, Southern California). Secondary: affluent suburban markets in any climate where outdoor living investment is high and spring/summer demand is strong. Winter shutdown in cold climates is the main geographic constraint.
core_buyer: Homeowners 35-65 with household income $150K+ who have recently invested in landscaping, outdoor living spaces, or pool installation. Secondary: commercial properties (restaurants, hotels, retail) wanting to enhance evening ambiance.
revenue_engine: Installation projects drive 80-90% of revenue. Maintenance contracts add 10-20% as recurring revenue. Referrals from landscape contractors and real estate agents generate leads. Dusk demonstrations (temporary lighting setup showing the result) close at 20-40%.
hard_part: Design skill is the hardest part to develop. Good landscape lighting is about artistry (which trees to uplight, beam spread, color temperature, layering) not just technical installation. The second challenge is lead generation in a fragmented market with no dominant marketing channel for lighting specifically.
why_path: DIY makes sense because startup costs are low ($5-15K), no license is required for low-voltage work, and the business can be tested with a few installations before committing. Acquisition targets are rare because most landscape lighting businesses are owner-operated solo operations. Franchise models in this space are weak.
economics_intro: Landscape lighting project economics are strong. A typical residential installation of $5,000 has $1,500 in materials (fixtures, transformer, wire), $1,000-1,500 in labor (1-2 days for 2 people), leaving $2,000-2,500 gross profit per project. At 3-4 installations per week during peak season, that is $6,000-10,000 weekly gross profit.
econ_insight: The critical metric is revenue per crew per day. A two-person crew that completes one $5,000 installation per day generates $1.25M annually if working 250 days. But weather, lead flow, and seasonal demand make that unrealistic. Target 3-4 installations per week during peak season (April-October) and 1-2 per week off-peak.
econ_stats: Material cost as pct of project
econ_rows: Completed project value per crew per day
Below 15% close rate = demo or pricing issue
Materials as pct of project revenue
Pct of install customers adding maintenance
Peak month vs off-peak month revenue
unit_econ_intro: Three-case model for a landscape lighting operation. Downside is a solo operator in a seasonal market. Base case is a two-person crew in a warm climate. Upside adds commercial projects and maintenance contracts.
buybox: Target market with $150K+ median household income
Warm climate or strong spring/summer outdoor living culture
Existing relationships with landscape contractors for referrals
Vehicle capable of carrying materials and equipment
Design eye for lighting aesthetics (can be developed with training)
$5-15K startup capital for tools, initial inventory, and marketing
disqualifiers: Cold climate with 5+ month outdoor off-season. No access to affluent neighborhoods. Unable to physically perform outdoor installation work. No design sensibility and unwilling to learn.
competitive_intro: Competition comes from general landscapers offering lighting as an add-on, licensed electricians doing outdoor work, dedicated landscape lighting companies (few per market), big-box store DIY lighting kits, and online retailers (Lumens, YLighting) selling direct to consumer. The advantage of a dedicated lighting specialist is design expertise, quality fixtures (not big-box grade), and professional installation with warranty.
comp_whitespace_summary: Whitespace in smart lighting integration (app-controlled, color-changing, scheduled scenes), commercial properties (restaurants, hotels wanting evening ambiance), and maintenance contracts (most installers sell and forget).
comp_rows: Lighting is an afterthought, limited design skill, commodity fixtures
Expensive, not design-oriented, not landscape specialists
Few per market, often booked out, premium pricing
Poor quality, no design, professional results impossible
Limited product, no installation, no design advice
valuation: Landscape lighting businesses rarely sell as standalone entities — they are usually rolled into landscape or electrical businesses. If profitable, expect 2-3x SDE. This is a cash flow business, not an exit-driven play.
financing: No financing needed for startup ($5-15K). Growth can be self-funded through project revenue. Vehicle financing if needed. Equipment leasing for specialty tools.
capital_rules: Start with $5K minimum. Never buy premium fixtures on speculation — order against signed contracts. Never hire a crew member before you have 3+ months of consistent project backlog. Stage every investment against confirmed demand.
cust_diligence: Dusk demonstration test: set up temporary lights at 50 homes, track conversion
Price sensitivity survey: what would homeowners pay for a complete system?
Ask landscape contractors: do they refer lighting work? To whom?
Check online reviews for existing lighting companies in target market
Assess seasonal demand patterns from local landscape companies
oper_diligence: Research state licensing requirements for low-voltage landscape lighting
Identify wholesale fixture suppliers (Unique Lighting, FX Luminaire, Kichler)
Calculate typical project timelines: design, procurement, installation hours
Assess tool and equipment requirements for professional installation
Research smart lighting systems (Lutron, Control4, app-based) for integration services
fin_diligence: Build per-project cost model: materials, labor, overhead per typical installation
Model seasonal cash flow: 70-80% of revenue in 6-7 months
Calculate break-even: how many projects per month cover fixed costs?
Estimate lead generation cost per demo and per closed project
Plan working capital for material purchases before customer payment
growth_diligence: Maintenance contracts: convert install customers to annual service agreements
Commercial projects: restaurants, hotels, retail outdoor spaces
Smart lighting integration: higher-margin technology add-on
Referral partnerships: landscape architects, pool builders, real estate agents
Product line: proprietary fixture designs or exclusive distribution
ai_intro: AI helps with lighting design (generating layout plans from property photos), project estimation (automated quoting from satellite imagery), and marketing (generating before/after content and targeting affluent homeowners).
ai_advantage: Design assistance is the biggest AI opportunity. An AI tool that takes a property photo and generates an optimized lighting plan (fixture placement, beam angles, color temperature) could dramatically reduce design time and improve consistency across projects.
ai_rows: AI-generated lighting plans from property photos
Design time reduction from 4 hours to 30 minutes
Automated quoting from satellite imagery and property data
Estimating time reduction and accuracy improvement
Route and crew optimization for installations
Predicting fixture failure and maintenance needs
risks: Seasonal demand: revenue drops to near-zero in cold-weather off-season
Project-based revenue is lumpy and unpredictable month-to-month
Low barrier to entry means constant competition from new entrants
Weather delays: rain and snow push project schedules and cash flow
Material cost increases: copper wire and LED fixture prices fluctuate
Liability: property damage during burial, electrical issues, trip hazards
Design dependency: bad design work generates bad referrals
kill_criteria: Cannot generate 3+ demos per week in target market. Close rate below 10% on demonstrations. Gross margin below 35% on installations. Cannot find reliable wholesale supplier. Off-season cash reserves insufficient for 4+ month gap.
market_text: The US landscape lighting market is $3B+ and growing 12-15% annually driven by the outdoor living trend, smart home integration, and LED technology improvements. The market is highly fragmented with no dominant national players. Most installations are done by general landscapers as an add-on service, creating whitespace for dedicated specialists with superior design skills. Low-voltage systems (under 30 volts) do not require an electrical license in most states, keeping the barrier to entry low.
market_trend: Strong positive. Outdoor living investment is at record levels. Smart home technology is driving lighting upgrades. LED quality and efficiency improvements are expanding the addressable market. The trend toward professional outdoor spaces (inspired by social media and home improvement shows) is generating demand for high-end installations.
op_wins: Low startup cost ($5-15K) with no license required
High gross margins (40-55%) on installation projects
Visual work generates strong word-of-mouth referrals
Maintenance contracts create recurring revenue from install base
Smart lighting integration is a growing premium service
op_losses: Starting in a cold-weather market without planning for off-season
Underpricing projects to win volume (destroys margin)
Using cheap fixtures that fail and generate warranty calls
Skipping the dusk demonstration (the strongest sales tool)
Not building referral partnerships with landscape contractors
Ignoring commercial opportunities in pursuit of residential only
op_quote: The demonstration is everything. Once a homeowner sees their property beautifully lit at dusk, the emotional sale is made. The operators who skip demos to save time are the ones who struggle with lead conversion.
comp_whitespace: Smart lighting integration: app-controlled, color-changing, scheduled scenes as premium add-on
Commercial properties: restaurants with patios, hotels, retail wanting evening ambiance
Maintenance contracts: most installers sell and forget, leaving recurring revenue on the table
New construction partnerships: builders wanting lighting as a standard upgrade
Holiday lighting crossover: seasonal service using same skills and customer base
Solar lighting solutions: growing segment for areas without easy electrical access
score_yes: Low startup cost and no license requirement
Growing demand from outdoor living trend
score_caveats: Seasonal demand in cold-weather markets is a major constraint
Project-based revenue is lumpy and unpredictable
Low barrier to entry creates constant competition
Score may overweight demand trend versus operational challenges
No natural recurring revenue without maintenance contracts

roadmap30: Purchase starter kit: $3-5K in demo fixtures, transformer, and burial wire
Complete manufacturer training (Unique Lighting or FX Luminaire free online courses)
Offer free dusk demonstrations to 50 homes in affluent target neighborhood
Install first 5 systems at competitive pricing to build portfolio
Track: demo conversion rate, project margins, customer satisfaction
kill30: Demo conversion below 15%. Cannot generate 5 demos per week. Gross margins below 35%. Target market response is lukewarm.
roadmap60: Establish wholesale accounts with 2-3 fixture suppliers
Hire first crew member for installations
Build referral partnerships with 5+ landscape contractors
Launch Google Business Profile and targeted local advertising
Develop maintenance contract offering for existing customers
Create project proposal template and pricing calculator
kill60: Cannot maintain 3+ installations per week. Gross margins declining. Crew hire does not work out. Lead generation cost unsustainable.
roadmap90: Make scale decision based on 8 weeks of project data
If scaling: invest in second crew vehicle and hire second installer
Launch commercial lighting offering for restaurants and retail
Implement maintenance contract program targeting 50%+ attachment rate
Explore smart lighting integration as premium service
Revenue target: $20K+/month during peak season or reassess
kill90: Revenue stalls below $15K/month during peak season. Maintenance contract attachment below 20%. Cannot sustain crew through off-season. Shelve and redirect to higher-conviction categories.
final_rec: Landscape lighting is a solid DIY test for warm-climate markets. Low startup cost, high margins, and visual referral dynamics make it worth a $5K pilot. The cold-weather seasonal constraint is real — this only works as a year-round business in FL, TX, or SoCal. If Dan is in a warm market and willing to test, the dusk demonstration is the killer sales tool. Otherwise, allocate time to higher-conviction acquisition plays.
next_actions: Buy $3-5K demo kit and complete manufacturer training
Test dusk demonstrations in target neighborhood
Track conversion rate and project margins obsessively
Build referral relationships with local landscape contractors
Make honest pursue/shelve decision at 30 days based on data
dan_decision: Is Dan in a warm-climate market where landscape lighting works year-round, or should he focus this budget and time on higher-conviction acquisition plays with less seasonality?
